title: Asymptote
short: A line the graph approaches but never quite reaches.
full:
An asymptote is a straight line that a curve gets arbitrarily close to as you move far along the graph. Rational functions have three kinds.

A vertical asymptote occurs where the denominator is zero but the numerator is not — the graph shoots off to ±∞ there. A horizontal asymptote describes the height the graph levels off to as x → ±∞. An oblique (slant) asymptote is a tilted line the graph follows when the numerator's degree is exactly one more than the denominator's.

A cancelled factor gives a hole instead of a vertical asymptote: a single missing point rather than an infinite break.
